title: Account Infrastructure Panorama
description: High-level overview of all infrastructure in an AWS account — resource counts, health signals, and top findings across compute, databases, serverless, and containers.
when: First time reviewing an account, onboarding, or preparing a health check.
prompt:
<role>
You are a senior cloud operations engineer performing an infrastructure assessment.
</role>

<context>
Cloud operations review — assess the overall infrastructure footprint and health of this AWS account to prepare a health check report.
</context>

<task>
Produce a structured summary covering these services:
1. EC2 — total instances by state (running/stopped). Identify any stopped longer than 7 days.
2. RDS/Aurora — all clusters with engine, version, and Multi-AZ status. Identify versions approaching end of support.
3. Lambda — total functions grouped by application prefix. Identify deprecated runtimes (python3.8, python3.9, nodejs16.x, nodejs18.x, java11, dotnet6 or older).
4. ECS/EKS — clusters with service count and health status.
5. CloudWatch — alarms in ALARM or INSUFFICIENT_DATA state with metric and threshold.
</task>

<output>
For each service: count + top 3 findings sorted by severity (CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM).
Conclude with a 3-sentence executive summary of account health.
State total resource count per service to confirm completeness.
</output>

<quality>
Paginate through all results for every service. Verify attributes via API calls.
Report only verified data — state limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
Before presenting, verify that total counts are consistent across your findings.
</quality>
expects: Structured summary with findings by severity and executive summary.

title: EC2 Fleet Review with Findings
description: Inventory all EC2 instances with security, cost, and modernization findings.
when: Reviewing instances for rightsizing, cleanup, or security assessment.
prompt:
<role>
You are a senior cloud operations engineer performing an infrastructure assessment.
</role>

<context>
EC2 fleet review — identify idle resources, security exposure, and modernization opportunities.
</context>

<task>
List all EC2 instances with: instance ID, name tag, instance type, state, public IP, launch time.
Sort by state (running first). Paginate through all results.
</task>

<scope>
After the inventory table, analyze and report:
- CRITICAL: instances with public IPs — verify if intentional or misconfiguration
- HIGH: instances stopped longer than 7 days — candidates for termination (still incurring EBS costs)
- MEDIUM: previous-generation instance types (m3, m4, t2, c4, r4) — candidates for modernization
</scope>

<output>
Inventory table, then findings by severity.
Summary line: total instances, running count, stopped count, percentage on current-generation types.
</output>

<quality>
Paginate through all results. Verify each attribute via describe-instances API.
Before presenting, confirm total instance count matches paginated results.
</quality>
expects: Instance table + security/cost/modernization findings.

title: Lambda Fleet Assessment
description: Identify Lambda functions needing runtime updates, Graviton migration, or memory right-sizing.
when: Runtime lifecycle review, cost optimization, or security assessment.
prompt:
<role>
You are a senior cloud operations engineer performing an infrastructure assessment.
</role>

<context>
Lambda fleet assessment — identify runtime risks, cost optimization opportunities, and modernization candidates.
</context>

<task>
List all Lambda functions grouped by application (infer application boundaries from naming prefix).
For each function: name, runtime, architecture (x86_64/arm64), memory allocation.
Paginate through every page until the complete list is retrieved.
</task>

<scope>
Analyze and categorize findings:
- CRITICAL: functions on deprecated or end-of-support runtimes (python3.8, python3.9, nodejs16.x, nodejs18.x, java11, dotnet6 or older)
- HIGH: functions on x86_64 architecture — eligible for ARM64 migration (~20% cost reduction). Note: verify runtime supports ARM64 before recommending.
- MEDIUM: functions on 128MB default memory — likely not right-sized for workload
- INFO: Java functions without SnapStart enabled — opportunity for cold start reduction
</scope>

<output>
Grouped inventory, then findings by severity.
Summary: total functions, percentage on deprecated runtimes, percentage x86 vs ARM64, percentage on default memory.
</output>

<quality>
Confirm total count matches the full paginated result.
Before presenting, verify percentages add up correctly.
</quality>
expects: Grouped inventory + runtime/architecture/memory findings with percentages.

title: Database Fleet Review
description: Assess all databases for version lifecycle risk, resiliency posture, and security baseline.
when: Version lifecycle review, resiliency assessment, or EOL analysis.
prompt:
<role>
You are a senior cloud operations engineer performing an infrastructure assessment.
</role>

<context>
Database fleet review — assess version lifecycle risk, resiliency posture, and security baseline.
</context>

<task>
List all RDS clusters and standalone instances with: identifier, engine, version, status, instance class, Multi-AZ configuration, encryption status.
Paginate through all results.
</task>

<scope>
Analyze and report:
- CRITICAL: engine versions within 6 months of end of standard support
- HIGH: clusters or instances without Multi-AZ (single point of failure)
- HIGH: instances without encryption at rest
- MEDIUM: previous-generation instance classes (db.m4, db.r4, db.t2) — Graviton alternatives available (db.m6g, db.r6g, db.m7g, db.r7g)
</scope>

<output>
Inventory table, then findings by severity. Confirm total database count.
</output>

<quality>
Paginate through all results. Verify each attribute via describe-db-clusters and describe-db-instances APIs.
Before presenting, confirm cluster count + standalone instance count = total.
</quality>
expects: Database inventory + version/resiliency/encryption findings.

title: Instance Network Path Trace
description: Trace the complete network path from a specific instance — VPC, subnet, routes, NAT, TGW, endpoints.
when: Troubleshoot connectivity or document network path for an instance.
prompt:
<role>
You are a senior cloud operations engineer performing an infrastructure assessment.
</role>

<context>
Network path analysis — trace connectivity from a specific instance to understand routing, internet access, and cross-VPC connectivity.
</context>

<task>
For instance [INSTANCE_ID], trace the complete network path:
1. Identify the VPC and subnet (include CIDR ranges)
2. Retrieve the route table associated with the subnet — list all routes
3. Determine internet access path: NAT Gateway, Internet Gateway, or none
4. Identify Transit Gateway or VPC Peering connections in the same VPC
5. List all VPC Endpoints available in the VPC
</task>

<output>
Present as a layered network path from the instance outward to the internet or peer VPCs.
</output>

<quality>
Verify each component exists via API before including it in the path.
Do not assume routing from subnet names or VPC tags.
</quality>
expects: Layered network path diagram from instance to internet/peers.

title: CloudWatch Alarms & Operational Health
description: Evaluate CloudWatch alarm state to identify active incidents, monitoring gaps, and overall health posture.
when: Preparing health check or understanding operational state.
prompt:
<role>
You are a senior cloud operations engineer performing an infrastructure assessment.
</role>

<context>
Operational health assessment — evaluate CloudWatch alarm state to identify active incidents, monitoring gaps, and overall health posture.
</context>

<task>
List all CloudWatch alarms grouped by state:
1. ALARM — requires attention
2. INSUFFICIENT_DATA — possible monitoring gaps or deleted resources
3. OK — healthy

For each alarm: name, metric, namespace, threshold, current state.
</task>

<output>
Grouped table by state (ALARM first), then assessment:
- Classify ALARM state: real incident vs expected behavior (e.g., auto-scaling target tracking)
- Classify INSUFFICIENT_DATA: missing metrics vs recently created alarms
- Overall health: percentage of alarms in OK state
- Confirm total alarm count.
</output>

<quality>
Paginate through all alarms. Verify current state via API — do not use cached values.
</quality>
expects: Alarm inventory grouped by state with health assessment.

title: ECS Cluster Architecture Review
description: Document all components of an ECS cluster and identify misconfigurations for operational readiness.
when: Documenting architecture, onboarding, or detecting misconfigurations.
prompt:
<role>
You are a solutions architect reviewing application architecture on AWS.
</role>

<context>
ECS cluster architecture review — document all components and identify misconfigurations for operational readiness.
</context>

<task>
Analyze ECS cluster [CLUSTER_NAME] end-to-end:
1. All services: status, desired/running task count, deployment state
2. Task definitions: container image, CPU, memory, port mappings
3. Target groups and load balancers — verify association via API
4. VPC, subnets, security groups
5. Auto-scaling configuration
</task>

<scope>
Identify misconfigurations:
- CRITICAL: services with 0 running tasks but desired > 0 (verify via describe-services)
- HIGH: target groups without associated load balancer
- MEDIUM: health check grace period set to 0 (tasks may be killed prematurely)
- MEDIUM: deployment circuit breaker disabled (failing services retry indefinitely — can cause death spirals and cost spikes in AWS Config)
</scope>

<output>
Summary table of services, then detailed findings by severity.
Confirm total service count.
</output>

<quality>
Paginate through all services. Verify each attribute via API — do not infer from names.
</quality>
expects: Service inventory + architecture findings by severity.

title: Lambda Integration Map
description: Map all connections for a Lambda function — triggers, destinations, IAM, network, layers.
when: Understanding blast radius before modifying, or documenting dependencies.
prompt:
<role>
You are a solutions architect reviewing application architecture on AWS.
</role>

<task>
Map all connections for Lambda function [FUNCTION_NAME].
Verify each connection via actual API calls — do not infer from function name or tags.

1. TRIGGERS: What invokes it? (API Gateway, EventBridge, S3, SQS) — show ARNs. Check list-event-source-mappings and get-policy for resource-based triggers.
2. DESTINATIONS: What does it connect to? Check BOTH the IAM policy actions AND environment variables for resource ARNs.
3. IAM: Execution role and all policies (list-attached-role-policies + list-role-policies + get-role-policy for each inline). Flag wildcards (*) in Action or Resource.
4. NETWORK: VPC, subnets, security groups (if applicable)
5. LAYERS: List with versions
</task>

<output>
Present as: what flows IN → function → what flows OUT
</output>

<quality>
Verify each connection via API. Do not infer from function name or tags.
</quality>
expects: Integration map showing triggers → function → destinations with IAM analysis.

title: Investigate API/Service Errors
description: Step-by-step investigation of API errors — check logs, configuration, backend resources, and alarms.
when: API returning errors, service degraded, customer-reported issue.
prompt:
<role>
You are a site reliability engineer investigating a production issue.
</role>

<context>
Incident investigation — [ENDPOINT] is returning [ERROR_CODE] errors. No deployments in the last 24 hours.
</context>

<task>
Investigate step by step — show findings at each step before moving to the next:

1. Check CloudWatch logs for [FUNCTION/SERVICE] — search for ERROR, Exception, AccessDenied in the last hour. If no errors in logs, check if the application is logging errors at all (this itself is a finding).
2. Check the function/service configuration — environment variables, IAM role, VPC config. Compare resource names in config against actual resources that exist.
3. Verify backend resources are accessible — does the DynamoDB table / RDS instance / S3 bucket actually exist with the exact name referenced?
4. Check active CloudWatch alarms for related services.
</task>

<output>
Findings at each step, then root cause diagnosis.
</output>

<quality>
Diagnose based ONLY on evidence found at each step.
If you cannot determine the cause from the evidence, state what additional information is needed.
</quality>
expects: Step-by-step investigation with evidence-based root cause.

title: ECS Service Health Check
description: Comprehensive health check of an ECS service — tasks, events, target group health, and resource utilization.
when: Service slow or returning errors, deployment issues, intermittent failures.
prompt:
<role>
You are a site reliability engineer investigating a production issue.
</role>

<context>
ECS service health assessment — determine if the service is healthy, degraded, or failing.
</context>

<task>
Check health of ECS service [SERVICE_NAME] in cluster [CLUSTER_NAME].
Verify each finding via actual API calls.

1. TASKS: all desired tasks running? Any in STOPPED state? Check stop reason via describe-tasks.
2. EVENTS: last 10 service events — any deployment failures or task placement errors?
3. TARGET GROUP: healthy vs unhealthy target count? Health check configuration (path, interval, threshold)? Verify via describe-target-health.
4. METRICS: CPU and memory utilization near limits? Check CloudWatch ECS metrics if available.
</task>

<output>
Verdict: HEALTHY, DEGRADED, or FAILING.
Most likely cause if not healthy.
If any API call fails or returns no data, state it explicitly.
</output>

<quality>
Verify via describe-services, describe-tasks, and describe-target-health APIs.
Before presenting verdict, confirm all 4 checks have been completed.
</quality>
expects: Health verdict with evidence from tasks, events, targets, and metrics.

title: EKS Cluster Health Check
description: Version lifecycle review, node group optimization, add-on status, and security posture for EKS clusters.
when: Version lifecycle review, node group optimization, security assessment.
prompt:
<role>
You are a container platform engineer assessing container infrastructure health.
</role>

<task>
For each EKS cluster in this account (paginate through all clusters):

1. VERSIONS: Cluster version, platform version. Is it within 3 months of end of standard support? Verify version via describe-cluster API.
2. NODE GROUPS: For each, show instance type, desired/min/max, AMI type, disk size. Paginate through all node groups.
3. ADD-ONS: List installed add-ons with versions. Flag any outdated.
4. LOGGING: Is control plane logging enabled? Which log types? Verify via describe-cluster API.
5. NETWORKING: VPC, subnets, security groups. Public or private endpoint? Verify endpoint access config.
</task>

<scope>
Flag:
- Clusters on versions approaching EOL
- Node groups using x86 instances (Graviton opportunity — ~20% savings)
- Node groups with min=max=desired (no auto-scaling configured)
- Public API endpoint enabled (security concern)
</scope>

<output>
Per-cluster summary, then findings by severity. Confirm total cluster and node group counts.
</output>

<quality>
Verify version via describe-cluster API — do not assume from cluster name.
</quality>
expects: EKS inventory + version/security/optimization findings.

title: ECS Services Health Scan — Death Spiral Detection
description: Scan all ECS services across clusters for failures, death spirals, and misconfigurations that generate hidden costs.
when: Detecting services in continuous failure, cost spikes from Config CIs, or org-wide ECS health review.
prompt:
<role>
You are a container platform engineer assessing container infrastructure health.
</role>

<task>
For each ECS cluster (list-clusters, paginate all), list all services (list-services, paginate all) and check via describe-services API:

1. HEALTH: desired count vs running count. Any service with running < desired?
2. FAILURES: For services not at desired count, check failedTasks count and last stopped task reason via describe-tasks.
3. DEATH SPIRAL: Services with high failedTasks count (>100) AND rolloutState=IN_PROGRESS for >24 hours — these are stuck in a loop of start→fail→restart, generating costs in Fargate compute and AWS Config CIs.
4. CIRCUIT BREAKER: Is deployment circuit breaker enabled? (if not, failing services retry forever)
5. HEALTH CHECK: What's the health check grace period? (0 = tasks may be killed too early)
</task>

<scope>
- CRITICAL: death spiral detected (high failedTasks + stuck rollout + circuit breaker disabled)
- HIGH: services with running < desired and no circuit breaker
- MEDIUM: circuit breaker disabled on any service
</scope>

<output>
For each failing service, estimate the daily cost of the failure loop (Fargate compute + Config CIs at $0.003/CI).
Confirm total cluster count and total service count across all clusters.
</output>

<quality>
Verify via describe-services API. Check failedTasks and rolloutState fields.
</quality>
expects: ECS health scan with death spiral detection and cost impact.

title: VPC Inventory with CIDR Overlap Detection
description: Map all VPCs, detect CIDR overlaps that prevent peering, and identify orphaned or test VPCs.
when: Network topology review, VPC peering planning, cleanup.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud networking specialist reviewing network infrastructure.
</role>

<context>
VPC topology assessment — map all VPCs and identify CIDR conflicts, orphaned resources, and governance gaps.
</context>

<task>
List all VPCs with: VPC ID, name tag, CIDR block, default (yes/no), number of subnets.
Paginate through all VPCs. Verify subnet count via describe-subnets API.
</task>

<scope>
Identify:
- CRITICAL: CIDR overlaps — VPCs with identical CIDR ranges (cannot be peered)
- HIGH: VPCs with no running resources (potential orphans — verify by checking for ENIs or instances)
- MEDIUM: VPCs with names suggesting test/demo purpose
- MEDIUM: the default VPC (should not host production workloads)
</scope>

<output>
Inventory table, then findings by severity.
Summary: total VPCs, CIDR ranges in use, overlap count.
</output>

<quality>
Verify each VPC's subnet count and resource presence via API.
Before presenting, confirm CIDR overlap detection covers all VPC pairs.
</quality>
expects: VPC inventory + CIDR overlap map + orphan candidates.

title: NAT Gateway Cost Optimization
description: Analyze NAT Gateway costs using CloudWatch metrics and identify free VPC Endpoint alternatives.
when: Networking cost review, VPC optimization.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud networking specialist reviewing network infrastructure.
</role>

<context>
NAT Gateway cost analysis — identify underutilized gateways and missing free VPC Endpoints.
</context>

<task>
1. List all NAT Gateways with: ID, VPC, subnet, state. Verify state via API.
2. For each NAT Gateway, check CloudWatch BytesOutToDestination and BytesInFromDestination (last 7 days). If <1GB/day, it may be underutilized ($32/mo for almost nothing).
3. Identify NAT Gateways not referenced by any route table (orphaned).
4. For each VPC with a NAT Gateway, check for S3 and DynamoDB Gateway Endpoints specifically. These are FREE and eliminate NAT data processing charges for those services.
</task>

<scope>
- CRITICAL: VPCs with NAT Gateway but no S3/DynamoDB Gateway Endpoint (free, immediate savings)
- HIGH: orphaned NAT Gateways not in any route table (pure waste)
- HIGH: NAT Gateways with 0 bytes processed in 7 days (idle)
- MEDIUM: multiple NAT Gateways in same VPC (verify HA vs redundant)
</scope>

<output>
Inventory, gap analysis, findings by severity.
Cost context: ~$32/month base + $0.045/GB processed. S3/DynamoDB Gateway Endpoints are free.
</output>

<quality>
Verify via describe-nat-gateways, describe-vpc-endpoints, and CloudWatch APIs.
Before presenting, confirm VPC-to-endpoint mapping is accurate.
</quality>
expects: NAT GW inventory + traffic analysis + VPC Endpoint gaps + cost savings.

title: Cost Overview & Trend Analysis
description: Complete AWS spending overview — total cost trend, top 10 services by cost with month-over-month changes and anomaly detection.
when: First time looking at costs, preparing for a FinOps meeting, or investigating a bill increase.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
Cost analysis — understand spending trajectory and identify the primary cost drivers in this AWS account.
Note: If this account is part of an AWS Organization, Cost Explorer may show consolidated costs. Specify if you want linked-account-only costs.
</context>

<task>
1. Retrieve total cost for the last 3 months, month by month using Cost Explorer API with UnblendedCost metric.
2. Identify the top 5 services by cost in the current month.
3. Calculate month-over-month change (%) for each top service.
</task>

<output>
Cost trend table (month, total), then top 5 services table (service, cost, MoM %).
Identify any service with >20% month-over-month increase as an anomaly.
Conclude with: Total monthly spend and biggest cost driver.
</output>

<quality>
Verify data via Cost Explorer API — report only actual cost data, not estimates from resource counts.
Before presenting, verify that service costs sum to approximately the total.
</quality>
expects: 3-month cost trend + top 5 services with MoM changes + anomaly flags.

title: Idle Resources Cleanup
description: Find idle resources costing money but providing no value — EC2, EBS, EIPs, ALBs, RDS, NAT Gateways.
when: Need quick savings with low risk, monthly cleanup routine.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
Idle resource identification — find resources incurring costs without providing business value.
</context>

<task>
Check each resource type via API. Paginate through all resources for each check.

1. EC2 instances stopped >7 days — check State and StateTransitionReason for stop date
2. Unattached EBS volumes — describe-volumes with status=available
3. Elastic IPs not associated with a running instance — describe-addresses, check AssociationId ($3.65/mo each)
4. Load balancers with 0 healthy targets — describe-target-health for each target group
5. RDS instances with 0 DatabaseConnections in last 7 days — check CloudWatch metric
6. NAT Gateways with 0 bytes processed in last 7 days — check CloudWatch BytesOutToDestination
7. EBS snapshots older than 90 days not linked to any AMI
</task>

<output>
For each finding: resource ID, region, estimated monthly cost, recommended action.
Total potential immediate savings at the end.
If you cannot verify a metric (e.g., CloudWatch data not available), state "unverified" — don't assume idle.
</output>

<quality>
Paginate through all resources. Verify idle status via API.
Mark uncertain items as "verify before deleting".
</quality>
expects: List of idle resources with cost estimates and total savings potential.

title: Graviton Adoption Assessment
description: Measure Graviton (ARM64) adoption across all compute services and estimate savings from migration.
when: Cost optimization review, Graviton migration planning.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
Graviton adoption assessment — measure current ARM64 usage and estimate savings from migrating eligible x86_64 resources.
</context>

<task>
Paginate through all resources for each service:
1. EC2 — list instances by architecture (x86_64 vs arm64). Calculate percentage on Graviton.
2. Lambda — list functions by architecture. Calculate percentage on ARM64. Note: verify runtime supports ARM64 before recommending.
3. RDS — list instances by instance class. Identify Graviton families (db.r6g, db.r7g, db.m6g, db.m7g, db.t4g).
4. ElastiCache — list clusters by node type. Identify Graviton families (cache.r6g, cache.r7g, cache.m6g, cache.m7g).
5. OpenSearch — list domains by instance type. Identify Graviton types (*.r6g.*, *.m6g.*, *.r7g.*).
</task>

<output>
Per-service breakdown, then summary:
- Total compute resources, percentage Graviton vs x86
- Estimated savings per service (~20% for EC2/Lambda, ~20% for RDS, ~10-20% for ElastiCache/OpenSearch)
- Top 5 easiest migrations (smallest blast radius)
</output>

<quality>
Verify architecture via API for each resource.
Before presenting, confirm percentages are mathematically correct.
</quality>
expects: Architecture breakdown per service + savings estimate + migration candidates.

title: Are My Logs Costing Too Much?
description: Analyze CloudWatch Logs for cost surprises — infinite retention, oversized log groups, and orphaned logs.
when: CloudWatch costs are unexpectedly high, or monthly cost review.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
CloudWatch Logs cost analysis — identify log groups with excessive storage costs due to retention, size, or abandonment.
</context>

<task>
1. List all log groups with: name, stored bytes, retention period (days or never expires).
2. Sort by stored bytes (largest first).
3. Calculate estimated monthly storage cost per log group ($0.03/GB/month).
Paginate through all log groups.
</task>

<scope>
Identify:
- CRITICAL: log groups with "never expires" retention (infinite storage growth)
- HIGH: log groups larger than 10GB (significant storage cost)
- MEDIUM: log groups with 0 incoming bytes in last 7 days (possibly orphaned)
</scope>

<output>
Table sorted by size, findings by severity.
Recommend retention policy: 7 days (dev), 30 days (staging), 90 days (prod).
Total potential savings if retention policies applied.
</output>

<quality>
Verify stored bytes via describe-log-groups API.
Before presenting, confirm total storage sums correctly across all groups.
</quality>
expects: Log group inventory sorted by cost + retention recommendations + savings estimate.

title: EC2 Rightsizing Analysis
description: Identify oversized EC2 instances using CloudWatch CPU metrics and recommend downsizing.
when: Monthly cost review, rightsizing initiative, Compute Optimizer follow-up.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
EC2 rightsizing analysis — identify instances where allocated compute exceeds actual utilization.
Note: Memory utilization requires CloudWatch Agent (not available by default). This analysis uses CPU only. For comprehensive rightsizing, also check AWS Compute Optimizer recommendations.
</context>

<task>
For each running EC2 instance, retrieve CloudWatch metrics (last 14 days):
- CPUUtilization: average and maximum
- NetworkIn/NetworkOut: average
</task>

<scope>
Identify candidates:
- CRITICAL: average CPU below 5% AND max CPU below 20% (strong downsize candidate)
- HIGH: average CPU below 10% (likely oversized)
- MEDIUM: previous-generation instance type (m4, c4, r4, t2)
</scope>

<output>
For each flagged instance:
| Instance ID | Name | Current Type | Avg CPU | Max CPU | Suggested Type | Est. Monthly Savings |

Total potential monthly savings.
Note: Memory metrics not available without CloudWatch Agent — flag instances where memory could be the bottleneck.
</output>

<quality>
Verify metrics via CloudWatch GetMetricStatistics API.
If CloudWatch data is unavailable for an instance, mark as "no metrics — enable detailed monitoring".
</quality>
expects: Rightsizing table with CPU evidence + suggested types + savings estimate.

title: EBS Volume Optimization
description: Find unattached volumes, GP2→GP3 migration candidates, oversized volumes, and old snapshots.
when: Storage cost review, monthly cleanup.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
EBS cost optimization — identify volumes and snapshots that are wasting money.
</context>

<task>
Paginate through all EBS volumes and snapshots. Check each via API.

1. UNATTACHED: volumes with status=available (no instance). Show volume ID, size, type, creation date, monthly cost.
2. GP2 to GP3: volumes still on gp2 type. GP3 is 20% cheaper with better baseline performance (3,000 IOPS, 125 MB/s). Migration is non-disruptive via ModifyVolume.
3. OVERSIZED: volumes larger than 500GB with less than 1 IOPS/day average (check CloudWatch VolumeReadOps + VolumeWriteOps, 14-day average).
4. OLD SNAPSHOTS: snapshots older than 90 days not associated with any AMI.
</task>

<output>
Findings by category with count and total monthly cost per category.
Grand total: potential monthly savings across all categories.
</output>

<quality>
Verify IOPS via CloudWatch VolumeReadOps/VolumeWriteOps API for oversized check.
Before presenting, confirm cost calculations match volume sizes and types.
</quality>
expects: 4-category EBS findings with cost per category + total savings.

title: RDS Cost Optimization
description: Find idle, oversized, or unoptimized RDS instances using CloudWatch metrics.
when: RDS is a top cost driver, monthly cost review.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
RDS cost optimization — RDS is frequently the #1 or #2 cost driver. Identify idle, oversized, and unoptimized instances.
</context>

<task>
Paginate through all RDS instances and Aurora clusters. Verify each metric via CloudWatch API.

1. IDLE: Instances with 0 DatabaseConnections in the last 7 days (average). Candidates for deletion or stop.
2. OVERSIZED: Instances with average CPU <10% over 14 days. Suggest smaller instance class.
3. GRAVITON: Instances on x86 classes (db.m5, db.r5, db.m6i, db.r6i) that could migrate to Graviton (db.m6g, db.r6g, db.m7g, db.r7g) for ~20% savings.
4. STORAGE: Instances with allocated storage >2x used storage (check FreeStorageSpace metric).
5. SINGLE-AZ: Production instances without Multi-AZ (risk flag, not cost saving).
</task>

<output>
For each finding: instance ID, current config, recommendation, estimated monthly savings.
If CloudWatch metrics are unavailable, state it — don't assume idle.
</output>

<quality>
Verify metrics via CloudWatch API. Do not estimate CPU from instance type.
</quality>
expects: RDS findings by category with savings estimates.

title: Lambda Cost Optimization
description: Find unused, over-provisioned, and unoptimized Lambda functions.
when: Lambda cost review, function cleanup.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<task>
Analyze all Lambda functions for cost optimization.
Paginate through ALL functions. Verify each finding via API.

1. UNUSED: Functions with 0 invocations in the last 30 days (CloudWatch Invocations metric). Candidates for deletion.
2. OVER-MEMORY: Functions where max memory used (CloudWatch MaxMemoryUsed) is <50% of allocated memory. Right-size the memory.
3. X86 → ARM64: Functions on x86_64 that could migrate to arm64 for ~20% savings. Verify runtime supports ARM64.
4. DEPRECATED RUNTIME: Functions on python3.8, python3.9, nodejs16.x, nodejs18.x, java11, dotnet6 or older.
5. HIGH DURATION: Functions with average duration >10 seconds. May benefit from optimization or architecture change.
</task>

<output>
For each: function name, current config, finding, recommendation.
Summary: total functions, % unused, % over-memory, % x86, potential savings.
</output>

<quality>
Verify metrics via CloudWatch API. If metrics unavailable, state it.
</quality>
expects: Lambda findings by category with savings estimates.

title: S3 Storage Class & Lifecycle Review
description: Analyze S3 buckets for missing lifecycle policies, suboptimal storage classes, and silent cost growth.
when: S3 costs growing, storage review, FinOps assessment.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
S3 cost optimization — identify buckets where storage class selection and lifecycle policies can reduce costs.
</context>

<task>
Paginate through all S3 buckets. For each bucket, check via API:

1. NO LIFECYCLE: buckets without lifecycle policy (data stays in Standard forever).
2. ALL STANDARD: buckets where 100% of data is in S3 Standard. If rarely accessed, Intelligent-Tiering or Standard-IA could save 40-60%.
3. LARGE BUCKETS: buckets larger than 100GB. Show size, storage class distribution, monthly cost estimate.
4. INCOMPLETE MULTIPART: check for incomplete multipart uploads (list-multipart-uploads). These consume storage silently.
5. NO VERSIONING CLEANUP: buckets with versioning enabled but no lifecycle rule to expire old versions.
</task>

<output>
Findings per category with bucket name, issue, estimated monthly cost, recommendation.
If bucket size cannot be determined without S3 Storage Lens, state the limitation.
</output>

<quality>
Verify each attribute via get-bucket-lifecycle-configuration and get-bucket-versioning APIs.
Before presenting, confirm bucket counts per category are accurate.
</quality>
expects: 5-category S3 findings with cost estimates and recommendations.

title: CloudTrail Cost Optimization
description: Find duplicate trails, expensive data events, and redundant multi-region configurations.
when: CloudTrail costs unexpectedly high, trail configuration review.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<context>
CloudTrail cost optimization — identify duplicate trails and expensive configurations.
</context>

<task>
List all trails via describe-trails API. For each trail, check via get-event-selectors:

1. DUPLICATE TRAILS: multiple trails logging the same events in the same region. Only 1 management trail per region is free — additional ones cost $2/100K events.
2. DATA EVENTS: trails with S3 or Lambda data events enabled. These can be very expensive at high volume.
3. MULTI-REGION: trails with IsMultiRegionTrail=true. One is good for compliance. Multiple is waste.
4. S3 DELIVERY: where are logs delivered? Multiple trails to different buckets means duplicate storage cost.
5. CLOUDWATCH LOGS: trails delivering to CloudWatch Logs. Check if the log group has subscription filters or metric filters. If none, the CW Logs destination is redundant (logs already go to S3).
</task>

<output>
Findings per category with trail name, configuration, estimated monthly cost impact, recommendation.
</output>

<quality>
Verify trail configuration via describe-trails and get-event-selectors APIs.
Before presenting, confirm trail count and configuration details are accurate.
</quality>
expects: Trail inventory with duplicate/cost findings and recommendations.

title: Data Transfer Cost Analysis
description: Break down data transfer costs by type — inter-AZ, inter-region, internet, NAT processing.
when: Data transfer is a confusing cost driver, networking cost review.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<task>
Analyze data transfer costs for the last 3 months:

1. Total data transfer cost by month (trend)
2. Break down by type: inter-AZ, inter-region, to internet, NAT Gateway processing
3. Top 5 resources generating the most data transfer cost
4. NAT Gateway data processing cost specifically
</task>

<scope>
Flag:
- Inter-AZ transfer that could be reduced with AZ-aware architecture
- NAT Gateway processing that could be avoided with VPC Endpoints
- Data transfer to internet that might benefit from CloudFront
</scope>

<quality>
Use Cost Explorer with UsageType dimension to separate transfer types.
</quality>
expects: Data transfer breakdown by type with optimization recommendations.

title: IAM Role Least-Privilege Audit
description: Analyze an IAM role for overly permissive policies and generate a least-privilege recommendation.
when: Quarterly security review, least-privilege initiative, pre-audit preparation.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud security engineer performing a security assessment.
</role>

<context>
IAM least-privilege review — assess a role's permissions and recommend the minimum required access.
Complement this analysis with IAM Access Analyzer for unused permissions detection.
</context>

<task>
Analyze IAM role [ROLE_ARN].
Verify all findings via API (list-attached-role-policies, list-role-policies, get-role-policy for each inline policy, get-policy-version for managed policies).

1. List all attached policies (managed and inline) — paginate if needed.
2. For each policy, read the actual policy document — identify: wildcards (*) in Action or Resource, overly broad permissions.
3. Check trust policy for multiple principals (role sharing).
</task>

<output>
Current state: risk table per policy (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW).
Recommended state: least-privilege version with only required actions and resources.
Show the diff between current and recommended.
</output>

<quality>
Read the actual policy document via get-policy-version API — permissions may differ from what the policy name suggests.
</quality>
expects: Policy risk assessment + least-privilege recommendation + diff.

title: Security Group Open Access Audit
description: Find security groups with overly permissive inbound rules (0.0.0.0/0) that expose resources to the internet.
when: Security assessment, compliance check, incident investigation.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud security engineer performing a security assessment.
</role>

<context>
Network security audit — identify security groups that expose resources to unrestricted internet access.
</context>

<task>
List all security groups with inbound rules allowing 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0.
For each: security group ID, name, VPC, the specific rule (port range, protocol), and what resources use this SG.
Paginate through all security groups.
</task>

<scope>
- CRITICAL: 0.0.0.0/0 on ports 22 (SSH) or 3389 (RDP) — direct admin access from internet
- HIGH: 0.0.0.0/0 on any port — unrestricted access
- MEDIUM: 0.0.0.0/0 on ports 80/443 only — may be intentional for web servers (verify)
</scope>

<output>
Findings table by severity with SG details and associated resources.
Total count of open security groups.
</output>

<quality>
Verify rules via describe-security-groups API.
Before presenting, confirm total count matches the sum of findings by severity.
</quality>
expects: Open SG inventory with severity classification and associated resources.

title: IaC Security Review
description: Review CloudFormation/Terraform templates for security issues before deployment.
when: Pre-deployment security check, code review.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud security engineer performing a security assessment.
</role>

<task>
Review templates at [PATH] for security issues.
For each check, read the actual template file — do not assume compliance from filename or directory.

- S3 without encryption or public access block
- Security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 on 22 or 3389
- IAM with wildcard permissions
- Resources without encryption at rest
- Missing IMDSv2 enforcement (HttpTokens: required)
- Hardcoded secrets or credentials
</task>

<output>
For each: | Severity | File:Line | Issue | Risk | Fix |

If a template uses parameters or conditions that make a check ambiguous, state "conditional — verify at deploy time" instead of assuming safe or unsafe.
Ask before auto-fixing.
</output>

<quality>
Verify all findings via API calls. Paginate through all results.
Report only verified data. State limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
</quality>
expects: Security findings table with severity, location, and fix.

title: Compliance Posture Snapshot
description: Quick compliance assessment across encryption, access, logging, network, and backup.
when: Preparing for audit, quarterly security review, new account assessment.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud governance engineer auditing compliance and governance.
</role>

<context>
Compliance posture assessment — evaluate this account against security baselines across five dimensions.
</context>

<task>
For each check, make the actual API call — verify compliance through data, not naming conventions.

1. ENCRYPTION: EBS volumes (Encrypted field), RDS (StorageEncrypted), S3 (get-bucket-encryption per bucket).
2. ACCESS: IAM users with console access (list-users + get-login-profile), root account MFA status (get-account-summary → AccountMFAEnabled).
3. LOGGING: CloudTrail enabled (describe-trails), VPC Flow Logs coverage per VPC (describe-flow-logs), S3 access logging.
4. NETWORK: Security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 inbound, public subnets with auto-assign public IP, public RDS instances, IMDSv2 enforcement.
5. BACKUP: AWS Backup plans (list-backup-plans), RDS backup retention (BackupRetentionPeriod > 0).
</task>

<output>
Per dimension: COMPLIANT | PARTIAL | NON-COMPLIANT with specific findings.
Overall score: X/5 dimensions compliant.
If any check fails or returns insufficient data, report it — don't skip.
</output>

<quality>
Verify every finding via API. Report findings even if they are positive (compliant).
Before presenting, verify that percentages are mathematically correct.
</quality>
expects: 5-dimension compliance scorecard with specific findings.

title: Tag Compliance Report
description: Check tag compliance across resource types for required tags. Tags are configurable.
when: Governance review, cost allocation audit, compliance check.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud governance engineer auditing compliance and governance.
</role>

<context>
Tag compliance assessment — verify that all resources have the required tags for cost allocation and governance.
Required tags: [Environment, Owner, CostCenter] — replace with your organization's required tags.
</context>

<task>
Check these resource types via API: EC2 (describe-instances), RDS (describe-db-clusters + describe-db-instances), Lambda (list-functions + list-tags per function), S3 (get-bucket-tagging per bucket), ECS (describe-clusters + list-tags-for-resource).

Paginate through all resources for each type.
</task>

<output>
For each resource type:
- Total resources
- Percentage with all required tags
- Percentage missing each specific tag
- List of non-compliant resources (ID + missing tags)

Overall tag compliance: X% of resources fully tagged.
Note: S3 get-bucket-tagging requires one API call per bucket — may be slow with 100+ buckets.
</output>

<quality>
Verify tags via actual API calls — do not infer tag presence from resource names.
Before presenting, confirm percentages are mathematically correct and totals match.
</quality>
expects: Per-resource-type tag compliance with non-compliant resource list.

title: Incident Triage — What Changed?
description: When something breaks, find what changed in the last 24 hours across IAM, infrastructure, config, and network.
when: Something broke and you don't know what changed.
prompt:
<role>
You are an incident responder performing triage and evidence collection.
</role>

<context>
Incident triage — something broke in the last 24 hours. Identify what changed.
</context>

<task>
Check CloudTrail for the last 24 hours via LookupEvents API and categorize changes:
1. IAM changes: new roles, policy attachments, permission changes
2. Infrastructure changes: EC2 launches/terminations, security group modifications, RDS changes
3. Configuration changes: Lambda updates, ECS deployments, CloudFormation stack operations
4. Network changes: VPC, subnet, route table, NAT Gateway modifications
</task>

<output>
For each change: timestamp, who (IAM principal), what (API call), result.
Sort by timestamp (most recent first).
Focus on changes that could cause an outage — filter out read-only API calls.
</output>

<quality>
Verify via CloudTrail LookupEvents API. Report only write events.
Before presenting, confirm the timeline is sorted correctly (most recent first).
</quality>
expects: Chronological timeline of infrastructure changes categorized by type.

title: Post-Incident Evidence Collection
description: Collect timeline evidence from CloudTrail, CloudWatch, and service logs for RCA after an incident.
when: After an incident, building timeline for root cause analysis.
prompt:
<role>
You are an incident responder performing triage and evidence collection.
</role>

<task>
We had an incident between [START_TIME] and [END_TIME] affecting [SERVICE/ENDPOINT].

Build an incident timeline:
1. CloudTrail: all write events in that time window related to [SERVICE]
2. CloudWatch: alarm state changes in that window
3. ECS/Lambda: any deployments or configuration changes
4. CloudWatch Logs: error patterns in [LOG_GROUP] during the window
</task>

<output>
Present as a chronological timeline:
| Time | Source | Event | Who | Impact |

End with: probable root cause based on the evidence, and what to investigate further.
</output>

<quality>
Only include events verified via API. If a data source is unavailable, state it.
</quality>
expects: Chronological incident timeline with evidence-based root cause.

title: S3 Inventory & Classification
description: Inventory all S3 buckets grouped by purpose with cleanup candidates identified.
when: Storage audit, cost review, security assessment.
prompt:
<role>
You are a senior cloud operations engineer performing an infrastructure assessment.
</role>

<task>
List ALL S3 buckets grouped by purpose (infer from naming prefix).
Paginate through all results — confirm total bucket count at the end.
Only report attributes you have verified via API.

Group by:
- Logging/audit (cloudtrail, flowlogs, access-logs)
- Application data (frontend, code, deploy, exports)
- Analytics/data lake (athena, glue, sagemaker)
- Backup/DR (backup, resilience)
- Other/unknown

For each bucket show: name, creation date, region.

After grouping, flag:
- Buckets with names suggesting test/manual creation (launch-wizard, test, demo)
- Buckets older than 1 year that might be orphaned
- Multiple buckets that appear to serve the same purpose (duplicates)
</task>

<output>
Summary: total buckets, % by category, estimated cleanup candidates.
</output>

<quality>
Verify all findings via API calls. Paginate through all results.
Report only verified data. State limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
</quality>
expects: Categorized bucket inventory with cleanup candidates.

title: S3 Security Audit
description: Verify all S3 buckets have encryption, public access block, versioning, and lifecycle policies.
when: Security assessment, compliance check.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud security engineer performing a security assessment.
</role>

<task>
For each S3 bucket in this account, check:
1. Is Block Public Access enabled? (all 4 settings should be true)
2. Is default encryption configured? (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS)
3. Is versioning enabled?
4. Is there a lifecycle policy?

Check EVERY bucket — paginate through the full list. For each check, make the actual API call (GetPublicAccessBlock, GetBucketEncryption, GetBucketVersioning, GetBucketLifecycleConfiguration). If an API returns an error (e.g., NoSuchPublicAccessBlockConfiguration), that means the setting is NOT configured — report it as non-compliant, don't skip it.
</task>

<output>
Show results as a table: Bucket | Public Access Block | Encryption | Versioning | Lifecycle
Flag any bucket that fails any check.
Summary: % compliant across all 4 checks.
</output>

<quality>
Verify all findings via API calls. Paginate through all results.
Report only verified data. State limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
</quality>
expects: S3 security compliance table with per-bucket results.

title: Kiro Steering Files Generator
description: Create Kiro steering files customized for your team's standards and conventions.
when: Setting up Kiro for a new team or project.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud platform engineer creating governance guardrails.
</role>

<task>
Help me create Kiro steering files for my team.

Ask me these questions one at a time, then generate the files:
1. Company name and industry?
2. Main workloads on AWS?
3. Preferred languages and runtimes?
4. IaC tool (CloudFormation/Terraform/CDK)?
5. Required tags on all resources?
6. Naming convention?
7. Compliance requirements?

Generate: product.md, tech.md, structure.md for the .kiro/steering/ folder.
</task>

<quality>
Verify all findings via API calls. Paginate through all results.
Report only verified data. State limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
</quality>
expects: Three steering files customized for the team.

title: Web App IaC Generator
description: Generate a complete CloudFormation/Terraform/CDK template for a serverless web application.
when: Prototypes, POCs, demos, or bootstrapping new projects.
prompt:
<role>
You are an infrastructure engineer generating production-ready IaC templates.
</role>

<task>
Create a CloudFormation template for a web application:

<requirements>
- API Gateway HTTP API with CORS
- Lambda function in Python 3.12, ARM64
- DynamoDB table for [YOUR_DATA_MODEL]
- IAM roles with least privilege only
</requirements>

<constraints>
- Tags: Environment=[ENV], Owner=[TEAM], CostCenter=[CC]
- Lambda: 256MB memory, 15s timeout
- DynamoDB: PAY_PER_REQUEST
- Include Outputs for API endpoint URL
</constraints>

Generate the template (YAML) and the Lambda code.
</task>

Variant: Replace "CloudFormation template" with "Terraform configuration" or "CDK stack in TypeScript" as needed.

<quality>
Verify all findings via API calls. Paginate through all results.
Report only verified data. State limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
</quality>
expects: Complete IaC template + Lambda code ready to deploy.

title: Cost Governance SCP Generator
description: Generate Service Control Policies to prevent cost waste at the organizational level.
when: Preventing waste org-wide, governance setup.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud platform engineer creating governance guardrails.
</role>

<task>
Create an SCP enforcing:
1. EBS: GP3 only (deny GP2 creation)
2. EC2: Graviton families only for new launches
3. Tags required: Environment, Owner, CostCenter
4. Regions: only [REGION_1] and [REGION_2]
5. Deny NAT Gateway creation outside networking account

Generate complete JSON. Explain each statement in plain English.
</task>

<quality>
Verify all findings via API calls. Paginate through all results.
Report only verified data. State limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
</quality>
expects: Complete SCP JSON with plain-English explanations.

title: Compute Optimizer Recommendations Review
description: Review AWS Compute Optimizer recommendations for rightsizing and Graviton migration.
when: Monthly cost review, rightsizing initiative.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps engineer analyzing AWS costs and efficiency.
</role>

<task>
Get Compute Optimizer recommendations for this account.
Paginate through all recommendations. Verify each recommendation exists via API — do not fabricate savings estimates.

Table: Resource ID, Type, Current Config, Recommended, Monthly Savings.
Sort by savings (highest first).

Group by type: Rightsizing, Graviton migration, Storage optimization.
Identify top 3 quick wins (highest savings, lowest effort).
Total potential monthly savings at the end.
If Compute Optimizer is not enrolled, state it — don't return empty results without explanation.
</task>

<quality>
Verify all findings via API calls. Paginate through all results.
Report only verified data. State limitations if any API returns incomplete results.
</quality>
expects: Prioritized recommendations with savings estimates.

title: Backup & DR Readiness Assessment
description: Assess backup coverage, RPO/RTO gaps, and disaster recovery readiness across all resource types.
when: DR planning, compliance audit, post-incident review, or quarterly resilience check.
prompt:
<role>
You are a reliability engineer assessing backup and disaster recovery readiness.
</role>

<context>
Backup & DR assessment — identify resources without backup protection and evaluate RPO/RTO gaps.
</context>

<task>
1. List all AWS Backup plans with: name, frequency, retention, target resources. Use backup.ListBackupPlans and backup.ListProtectedResources APIs.
2. Cross-reference against all EC2 instances, RDS clusters, EBS volumes, DynamoDB tables, and EFS file systems.
3. Identify resources NOT covered by any backup plan.
4. For covered resources, evaluate if backup frequency meets a 1-hour RPO target.
5. Check for cross-region backup copies (backup vaults in secondary region).
</task>

<output>
Backup coverage table: Resource Type | Total | Protected | Unprotected | Coverage %
Gap analysis: resources without backup, sorted by criticality.
RPO assessment: backup frequency vs target RPO.
DR readiness: cross-region backup status.
</output>

<quality>
Verify protection status via API — do not assume a resource is protected based on tags alone.
Paginate through all resources for each service.
</quality>
expects: Backup coverage matrix + unprotected resources + RPO/RTO gap analysis.

title: End-of-Support Version Scan
description: Scan all managed services for versions approaching or past end of standard support — RDS engines, EKS clusters, Lambda runtimes, ElastiCache, OpenSearch.
when: Quarterly version lifecycle review, upgrade planning, or compliance check.
prompt:
<role>
You are a platform engineer performing a version lifecycle review to identify end-of-support risks.
</role>

<context>
Version lifecycle scan — identify resources running versions that are approaching or past end of standard support, which may incur extended support charges or lose security patches.
</context>

<task>
For each service, list all resources with their current version and check against known EOL dates:
1. RDS/Aurora — engine version for all clusters and instances. Flag versions within 6 months of end of standard support.
2. EKS — cluster Kubernetes version. Flag versions within 4 months of end of standard support.
3. Lambda — runtime for all functions. Flag deprecated or end-of-support runtimes.
4. ElastiCache — engine version for all clusters. Flag versions approaching EOL.
5. OpenSearch — domain engine version. Flag versions approaching EOL.
Paginate through all resources for each service.
</task>

<output>
Per-service table: Resource | Current Version | EOL Date | Status (OK / Warning / Critical)
Summary: total resources scanned, % at risk, upgrade priority list.
</output>

<quality>
Verify versions via describe APIs. Check AWS documentation for current EOL dates.
If EOL date is uncertain, state it and recommend checking the official version lifecycle page.
</quality>
expects: Per-service version inventory with EOL status and upgrade priority list.

title: Multi-AZ Resilience Check
description: Identify single-AZ deployments across compute, database, and networking — the most common cause of avoidable outages.
when: Resilience review, Well-Architected assessment, or post-AZ-outage analysis.
prompt:
<role>
You are a reliability engineer identifying single points of failure caused by single-AZ deployments.
</role>

<context>
Multi-AZ resilience check — single-AZ deployments are the most common cause of avoidable outages during AZ impairments.
</context>

<task>
Check each service for AZ distribution:
1. EC2 — group running instances by AZ. Flag if >80% of instances are in a single AZ.
2. RDS/Aurora — check MultiAZ status for all clusters and instances. Flag single-AZ production databases.
3. ELB — check subnets for each load balancer. Flag ALBs/NLBs in fewer than 2 AZs.
4. ECS — for each service, check task placement across AZs. Flag services with all tasks in one AZ.
5. ElastiCache — check cluster mode and AZ distribution. Flag single-AZ replication groups.
</task>

<output>
Per-service AZ distribution table.
Findings by severity: CRITICAL (production single-AZ), HIGH (non-production single-AZ), MEDIUM (unbalanced).
Summary: % of resources with multi-AZ protection.
</output>

<quality>
Verify AZ placement via describe APIs. Do not infer AZ from subnet names — check actual placement.
</quality>
expects: AZ distribution per service + single-AZ findings by severity.

title: GuardDuty Findings Triage
description: Triage active GuardDuty findings by severity — identify critical threats, group by type, and provide remediation guidance.
when: Security review, incident response, or weekly security hygiene check.
prompt:
<role>
You are a security operations engineer triaging GuardDuty findings to prioritize remediation.
</role>

<context>
GuardDuty findings triage — review active findings to identify real threats vs noise and prioritize response.
</context>

<task>
1. Get the GuardDuty detector ID via guardduty.ListDetectors.
2. Get finding statistics grouped by severity and type via guardduty.GetFindingsStatistics.
3. List findings filtered by severity (HIGH first, then MEDIUM) via guardduty.ListFindings.
4. For each HIGH severity finding, get details via guardduty.GetFindings — extract: type, resource, actor IP, count, first/last seen.
</task>

<output>
Summary: total findings by severity (High/Medium/Low).
High severity table: Finding Type | Resource | Actor | Count | First Seen | Recommended Action.
Top 3 remediation priorities with specific steps.
</output>

<quality>
Verify findings via API. If GetFindings is access-denied, report the limitation and provide statistics only.
Do not fabricate finding details — report only what the API returns.
</quality>
expects: Finding severity summary + high-priority findings table + remediation steps.

title: Credentials & Secrets Hygiene
description: Audit IAM access keys age, unused credentials, root account status, and secrets rotation compliance.
when: Quarterly security review, compliance audit, or credential rotation initiative.
prompt:
<role>
You are an IAM security specialist auditing credential hygiene and secrets management.
</role>

<context>
Credentials audit — identify stale access keys, unused credentials, and secrets rotation gaps that increase breach risk.
</context>

<task>
1. Generate IAM credential report via iam.GenerateCredentialReport then iam.GetCredentialReport.
2. From the report, identify:
   - Access keys older than 90 days (flag >180 days as CRITICAL)
   - Access keys never used
   - Users with console access but no MFA
   - Root account: MFA status, access key presence
3. List Secrets Manager secrets via secretsmanager.ListSecrets — check rotation status and last rotated date.
4. Check SSM Parameter Store for SecureString parameters without rotation.
</task>

<output>
Credential hygiene scorecard:
| Check | Status | Count | Finding |
Access keys age distribution chart.
Top remediation priorities.
</output>

<quality>
Verify via credential report API — do not infer from user names.
If credential report generation takes time, wait and retry.
</quality>
expects: Credential hygiene scorecard + stale keys list + rotation gaps.

title: Savings Plans & RI Coverage Analysis
description: Analyze Savings Plans and Reserved Instance coverage — find on-demand waste and recommend optimal commitments.
when: FinOps review, commitment planning, or RI/SP expiration approaching.
prompt:
<role>
You are a FinOps analyst evaluating compute commitment coverage to minimize on-demand waste.
</role>

<context>
Savings Plans & RI coverage analysis — identify on-demand spend that could be covered by commitments for 20-60% savings.
</context>

<task>
1. Use Cost Explorer GetSavingsPlansCoverage API — last 30 days, daily granularity. Calculate average coverage %.
2. Use Cost Explorer GetSavingsPlansUtilization API — verify existing SPs are being fully utilized.
3. Use Cost Explorer GetReservationCoverage API — check RI coverage for EC2, RDS, ElastiCache.
4. Use Cost Explorer GetSavingsPlansPurchaseRecommendation API — get AWS recommendations for new commitments.
5. Identify on-demand spend not covered by any commitment.
</task>

<output>
Coverage summary: SP coverage %, RI coverage %, on-demand %.
Utilization: are existing commitments fully used?
Recommendation: suggested new commitments with estimated savings.
Expiring commitments in next 30/60/90 days.
</output>

<quality>
Use Cost Explorer APIs — do not estimate from instance counts.
Verify utilization data before recommending new purchases.
</quality>
expects: Coverage/utilization summary + on-demand waste + purchase recommendations.

title: DynamoDB Cost & Configuration Review
description: Review DynamoDB tables for capacity mode optimization, unused tables, expensive GSIs, and missing auto-scaling.
when: DynamoDB costs growing, capacity planning, or FinOps review.
prompt:
<role>
You are a database cost optimization specialist reviewing DynamoDB table configurations.
</role>

<context>
DynamoDB optimization — identify tables where capacity mode, GSI configuration, or auto-scaling settings are wasting money.
</context>

<task>
List all DynamoDB tables via dynamodb.ListTables, then dynamodb.DescribeTable for each:
1. CAPACITY MODE: tables in Provisioned mode with low utilization — candidates for On-Demand.
2. UNUSED TABLES: tables with 0 read/write operations in last 14 days (check CloudWatch ConsumedReadCapacityUnits + ConsumedWriteCapacityUnits).
3. GSI WASTE: Global Secondary Indexes with 0 reads in last 14 days — paying for unused indexes.
4. AUTO-SCALING: Provisioned tables without auto-scaling configured.
5. BACKUP: tables without point-in-time recovery (PITR) enabled.
Paginate through all tables.
</task>

<output>
Table inventory with capacity mode, RCU/WCU, GSI count, PITR status.
Findings by category with estimated monthly cost per finding.
Total potential savings.
</output>

<quality>
Verify utilization via CloudWatch metrics — do not assume from provisioned capacity alone.
Paginate through all tables.
</quality>
expects: Table inventory + capacity/GSI/scaling findings + savings estimate.

title: CloudFront Distribution Review
description: Review CloudFront distributions for cache hit ratio, origin configuration, and cost optimization opportunities.
when: CDN cost review, performance optimization, or architecture assessment.
prompt:
<role>
You are a CDN specialist reviewing CloudFront distributions for performance and cost optimization.
</role>

<context>
CloudFront review — optimize cache hit ratio, identify misconfigured origins, and reduce data transfer costs.
</context>

<task>
1. List all CloudFront distributions via cloudfront.ListDistributions.
2. For each distribution: ID, domain, origins, status, price class, WAF association.
3. Check CloudWatch metrics (last 7 days): CacheHitRate, Requests, BytesDownloaded, 4xxErrorRate, 5xxErrorRate.
4. Identify optimization opportunities:
   - Cache hit ratio below 80% (caching not effective)
   - Distributions without WAF (security gap)
   - Price class ALL (paying for edge locations you may not need)
   - Origins pointing to S3 without OAC/OAI (direct access possible)
   - High 4xx/5xx error rates
</task>

<output>
Distribution inventory table.
Performance metrics per distribution.
Findings by severity with recommendations.
</output>

<quality>
Verify metrics via CloudWatch API. If metrics are unavailable, state the limitation.
</quality>
expects: Distribution inventory + cache performance + optimization findings.

title: Multi-Account Organization Overview
description: Map your AWS Organization — accounts, OUs, spend per account, and identify shadow accounts or cost anomalies.
when: Organization governance review, new TAM onboarding, or cost allocation audit.
prompt:
<role>
You are a cloud governance architect mapping an AWS Organization for visibility and cost accountability.
</role>

<context>
Organization overview — understand the account structure, identify cost drivers per account, and flag governance gaps.
</context>

<task>
1. List all accounts in the organization via organizations.ListAccounts. Show: account ID, name, email, status, joined date.
2. Use Cost Explorer to get last month's spend per account (GROUP_BY LINKED_ACCOUNT).
3. Identify:
   - Accounts with $0 spend (possibly unused)
   - Accounts with >20% MoM spend increase (anomaly)
   - Accounts without meaningful name (governance gap)
   - Accounts joined in last 30 days (new, verify purpose)
</task>

<output>
Account inventory sorted by spend (highest first).
Top 5 accounts by cost with MoM trend.
Governance findings.
Summary: total accounts, active vs inactive, total org spend.
</output>

<quality>
Paginate through all accounts. Verify spend via Cost Explorer API.
If Organizations API is access-denied, state the limitation.
</quality>
expects: Account inventory with spend + governance findings + anomalies.

title: Route 53 DNS Health & Hygiene
description: Audit Route 53 hosted zones, health checks, and DNS records for orphaned entries and missing health checks.
when: DNS review, domain migration, or operational hygiene check.
prompt:
<role>
You are a DNS operations engineer auditing Route 53 for hygiene and reliability.
</role>

<context>
Route 53 DNS audit — identify orphaned records, missing health checks, and configuration issues.
</context>

<task>
1. List all hosted zones via route53.ListHostedZones. Show: zone name, type (public/private), record count.
2. For each zone, list record sets via route53.ListResourceRecordSets.
3. List all health checks via route53.ListHealthChecks. Show: ID, type, target, status.
4. Identify:
   - CNAME or A records pointing to non-existent resources (orphaned)
   - Alias records to deleted ELBs, CloudFront distributions, or S3 buckets
   - Failover or weighted routing without health checks
   - Health checks in unhealthy status
</task>

<output>
Hosted zone inventory.
Health check status table.
Findings: orphaned records, missing health checks, unhealthy checks.
</output>

<quality>
Verify record targets exist by cross-referencing with ELB, CloudFront, and S3 APIs where possible.
Paginate through all zones and records.
</quality>
expects: Zone inventory + health check status + orphaned/misconfigured DNS findings.

title: Quick Well-Architected Assessment
description: Rapid 6-pillar Well-Architected assessment — one key check per pillar for a quick infrastructure health score.
when: Quick health check, new account review, or pre-meeting infrastructure snapshot.
prompt:
<role>
You are a solutions architect performing a rapid Well-Architected assessment across all six pillars.
</role>

<context>
Quick Well-Architected review — one high-impact check per pillar to produce a fast infrastructure health score.
</context>

<task>
Perform one key check per pillar via API:

1. OPERATIONAL EXCELLENCE: CloudWatch alarms — how many are in ALARM or INSUFFICIENT_DATA state? (cloudwatch.DescribeAlarms)
2. SECURITY: IAM — any users with console access but no MFA? Root account MFA status? (iam.GetCredentialReport)
3. RELIABILITY: RDS — what percentage of databases have Multi-AZ enabled? (rds.DescribeDBInstances)
4. PERFORMANCE: EC2 — what percentage of instances are on current-generation types? (ec2.DescribeInstances)
5. COST OPTIMIZATION: EBS — how many unattached volumes exist? (ec2.DescribeVolumes with status=available)
6. SUSTAINABILITY: EC2/Lambda — what percentage of compute is on Graviton (ARM64)? (ec2.DescribeInstances + lambda.ListFunctions)
</task>

<output>
Scorecard:
| Pillar | Check | Result | Score |
| Operational Excellence | Alarm health | 3/47 in ALARM | ⚠ |
| Security | MFA coverage | 2 users without MFA | ❌ |
...

Overall: X/6 pillars healthy.
Top 3 actions to improve score.
</output>

<quality>
Verify each check via the specified API. One check per pillar — keep it fast.
If any API fails, score that pillar as "Unable to assess" and move on.
</quality>
expects: 6-pillar scorecard with one check each + top 3 improvement actions.

title: ElastiCache Fleet Review
description: Review ElastiCache clusters for engine version, node type, Multi-AZ, encryption, and utilization.
when: Cache infrastructure review, version lifecycle check, or cost optimization.
prompt:
<role>
You are a caching infrastructure specialist reviewing ElastiCache clusters for health and optimization.
</role>

<context>
ElastiCache fleet review — assess engine versions, node sizing, resilience, and security configuration.
</context>

<task>
1. List all ElastiCache replication groups via elasticache.DescribeReplicationGroups.
2. List all standalone cache clusters via elasticache.DescribeCacheClusters.
3. For each: cluster ID, engine (Redis/Memcached), version, node type, number of nodes, Multi-AZ status, encryption at-rest, encryption in-transit, automatic failover.
4. Check CloudWatch metrics (last 7 days): CPUUtilization, EngineCPUUtilization, DatabaseMemoryUsagePercentage, CurrConnections.
5. Identify:
   - CRITICAL: engine versions approaching end of support
   - HIGH: clusters without Multi-AZ or automatic failover
   - HIGH: clusters without encryption (at-rest or in-transit)
   - MEDIUM: clusters with <10% memory utilization (oversized)
   - MEDIUM: previous-generation node types (cache.r5 → cache.r7g)
</task>

<output>
Cluster inventory table.
Findings by severity.
Summary: total clusters, % encrypted, % Multi-AZ, % current-gen.
</output>

<quality>
Verify all attributes via describe APIs. Paginate through all clusters.
</quality>
expects: Cluster inventory + version/resilience/security findings + utilization data.
